coredata.dc:title: Targeted delivery strategies: The interactions and applications of nanoparticles in liver diseases

coredata.dc:description: In recent years, nanoparticles have been broadly utilized in various drugs delivery formulations. Nanodelivery systems have shown promise in solving problems associated with the distribution of hydrophobic drugs and have promoted the accumulation of nanomedicines in the circulation or in organs. However, the injection dose of nanoparticles (NPs) is much greater than that needed by diseased tissues or organs. In other words, most of the NPs are localized off-target and do not reach the desired tissue or organs. With the rapid development of biodegradable and biosafety nanomaterials, the nanovectors represent assurance of safety. However, the off-target effects also induce concerns about the application of NPs, especially in the delivery of gene editing tools. Therefore, a complete understanding of the biological responses to NPs in the body will clearly guide the design of targeted delivery of NPs. The different properties of various nanodelivery systems may induce diverse interactions between carriers and organs. In this review, we describe the relationship between the liver, the most influenced organ of systemic administration of NPs, and targeted delivery nanoplatforms. Various transport vehicles have adopted multiple delivery strategies for the targeted delivery to the cells in the homeostasis liver and in diseased liver. Additionally, nanodelivery systems provide a novel strategy for treating incurable diseases. The appearance of a targeted delivery has profoundly improved the application of NPs to liver diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing mechanisms of immune surveillance in liver sinusoids. Resident hepatic cells (KC, DC, LSEC, NKT, HSC, and hepatocytes) and immune cells in the liver blood circulation (NK cells, B cells, T cells, and monocyte) monitor the liver immune microenvironment. KC: Kupffer cell; HSC: hepatic stellate cell; DC: Dendritic cell; LSEC: liver sinusoidal endothelial cell; NK: natural killer cell NKT: natural killer T cell.

Interactions between NPs and hepatic immune cells. Schematic illustration of strategies for nanodelivery systems targeting different immune cells in the liver; [21–23,26,28,31,34]. SVMAV: Self-assembling Vehicle-free Multi-component Antitumor nanoVaccine; NNPs: Neutrophil membrane-coated nanoparticles. EGFR: Epidermal growth factor receptor; KC: Kupffer cell; HSC: hepatic stellate cell; DC: Dendritic cell; LSEC: liver sinusoidal endothelial cell; NK: natural killer cell NKT: natural killer T cell; HCC: hepatocellular carcinoma.

Schematic illustration of the mechanism for α-melittin-NP-mediated suppression of liver metastasis by LSECs targeting delivery. α-melittin-NPs specifically modulate LSECs, which involves the increased expression levels of costimulatory molecules (CD80, CD86, and MHC-II) and the release of cytokines/chemokines (IL-1α, IL-18, CXCL9, CXCL10, etc.). The activation of NK and T cells inhibit liver metastasis; [36]. MHC-II: major histocompatibility complex class II; IL-18: Interleukin-18; IL-1α: Interleukin-1α; CXCL9: C-X-C motif chemokine ligand 9; CXCL10: C-X-C motif chemokine ligand 10; LSEC: liver sinusoidal endothelial cell; NK: natural killer cell; CTL: Cytotoxic T cell. Figure reprinted with permission. Copyright (2019) Nature Communications.

Schematic showing the HSCs targeted delivery by combined riociguat and IGNP-JQ1 treatment in the fibrotic liver. Recovery of sinusoid and liver porosity, enhancing the efficacy of IGNP-JQ1 transport into the Disse space. IGNP facilitates the targeted delivery of JQ1 to activated HSCs and reverse fibrosis; [40]. ECM: extracellular matrix; IGF-2R: insulin-like growth factor 2 receptor; sGC: soluble guanylate cyclase. Figure reprinted with permission. Copyright (2023) Wiley-VCH GmbH.
A summary of factors determining the fate of NPs for the targeted delivery in the body.

Schematic of nanodelivery systems applied to anti-HCC treatments. Chemotherapeutic drugs, targeted drugs, or nuclear acids drugs can be transport to cancer cell via modified LNPs; [54,55,113–115]. Metal NPs coated by polymer or peptides induce the apoptosis of HCC; [116,117]. CQDs are both utilized in tumor imaging and treatment; [118,119,121]. ZnS@BSA nanoclusters activate the cCAS/STING pathway and enhance T cell anti-tumor effects; [122]. DOX: Doxorubicin; DTX: Docetaxel; 5-Fu: 5-Fluorouracil; CQDs: Carbon quantum dots; BSA: bovine serum albumin; ASGP-R: asialoglycoprotein receptor.

Mechanisms of prevention of liver IRI by nanoantioxidants. Ceria NPs can efficiently scavenge ROS in KCs and LSECs. After Ceria NPs treatments, pro-inflammatory cytokines and recruited pro-inflammatory monocytes and neutrophils are reduced [142]. ROS: reactive oxygen species; IL-1: Interleukin-1; IL-6: Interleukin-6; IL-12: Interleukin-12; TNF-α: Tumor necrosis factor-α; INF-γ: Interferon-γ; NOS2: nitric oxide synthase 2; ICAM-1: intracellular adhesion molecule-1; MPO: myeloperoxidase; Figure reprinted with permission. Copyright (2023) Wiley-VCH GmbH.

Schematic of immune tolerance inducement on liver APCs and Tregs. Low expression of costimulatory molecules, MHC-II and CD11c, in the liver APCs facilitate the release of IL-10, IL-35, and TGF-β. Antigens are carried to the liver APCs (KCs, DCs or LSECs) by LNP or polymer NP to rebuild liver immune tolerance; [35,83,85,148]. Breg can be recruited by pMHCII-Iron oxide NPs and suppress the activation of APCs; [145]. Autoimmune hepatitis treated by ANANAS-Sc-Hz-Dex can significantly reduce the injection dose of Dex; [147]. IL-6: Interleukin-6; IL-10: Interleukin-10; IL-12: Interleukin-12; IL-35: Interleukin-35; Treg: Regulatory T cell; Breg: Regulatory B cell; MHC-II: major histocompatibility complex class II; TGF-β: Transforming growth factor beta; PD-1: Anti-programmed cell death 1; PD-L1: Anti-programmed cell death ligand 1; PGE2: Prostaglandin E2; Dex: dexamethasone.

Mechanism of NTLA-2001 LNP delivery of CRISPR-Cas9 tools in the treatment of transthyretin amyloidosis disease. In panel A, the human-optimized messenger RNA (mRNA) molecule encoding the Streptococcus pyogenes (Spy) Cas9 protein is delivered by the NTLA-2001 LNP to patient hepatocytes via ApoE binding in the serum strategy. TTR: transthyretin; ApoE: apolipoprotein E. In panel B, schematic of NLTA-2001 LNP uptake by hepatocytes through LDL receptor-mediated endocytosis. The released Cas9 mRNA is translated and forms a Cas9 ribonucleoprotein complex with sgRNA. LDL: low-density lipoprotein. In panel C, the Cas9 ribonucleoprotein complex recognizes the protospacer-adjacent motif (PAM) in TTR DNA by sgRNA. Endogenous DNA repair mechanisms ligate the ends of the cut, potentially introducing insertions or deletions of bases (indels); [48]. Figure reprinted with permission. Copyright (2021) Massachusetts Medical Society.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, nanoparticles have been broadly utilized in various drugs delivery formulations. Nanodelivery systems have shown promise in solving problems associated with the distribution of hydrophobic drugs and have promoted the accumulation of nanomedicines in the circulation or in organs. However, the injection dose of nanoparticles (NPs) is much greater than that needed by diseased tissues or organs. In other words, most of the NPs are localized off-target and do not reach the desired tissue or organs. With the rapid development of biodegradable and biosafety nanomaterials, the nanovectors represent assurance of safety. However, the off-target effects also induce concerns about the application of NPs, especially in the delivery of gene editing tools. Therefore, a complete understanding of the biological responses to NPs in the body will clearly guide the design of targeted delivery of NPs. The different properties of various nanodelivery systems may induce diverse interactions between carriers and organs. In this review, we describe the relationship between the liver, the most influenced organ of systemic administration of NPs, and targeted delivery nanoplatforms. Various transport vehicles have adopted multiple delivery strategies for the targeted delivery to the cells in the homeostasis liver and in diseased liver. Additionally, nanodelivery systems provide a novel strategy for treating incurable diseases. The appearance of a targeted delivery has profoundly improved the application of NPs to liver diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The liver is a prevalent organ for targeted nanodelivery systems, because of its unique functions and position, often leading to the accumulation of nanoparticles. The immune surveillance of liver is maintained by the majority of hepatic cells. Thus, understanding the relationships between NPs and hepatic cells will dramatically benefit the targeted delivery in liver diseases treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles (NPs) are novel drug delivery systems for the transportation of various types of drugs or imaging agents. Their unique properties can completely change the fate of drugs in the body. NPs act as professional vehicles that drive medicine to a diseased area by a prolonged circulation time and targeted accumulation of specific organs. The strategy of nanomaterial research is to develop NPs that are biodegradable, biocompatible, having a good biosafety profile. NPs range in size from 1 to 100 nm [1]. The classification of nanoparticles can be divided into five parts: inorganic-based NPs are precisely formulated and can be designed in various sizes, structures, and geometry, included quantum dot (QD), gold NPs, iron oxide and mesoporous silica; the applications of carbon-based NPs depended on their mechanical, electrical, thermal, and physicochemical properties as well as biological abilities, contained carbon quantum dot, Fullerene, Carbon nanotubes, and Graphene; lipid-based NPs are considered as ideal drug carriers, involved Liposome, Solid lipid nanoparticles, and Nanoemulsion; polymeric NPs showed the potential use for controlled release and ability to optimize therapeutic effects, covered Polymersome, Polymer micelle and Dendrimer; biomimetic NPs are formed by integrating different biomaterials onto surfaces, like cell membrane [2]. With the increasing demands of clinical treatments more and more NPs are becoming available. Several types of NPs have been introduced in clinical practice [3,4].

However, most NPs administered in the body will be eliminated; only a few will reach disease sites. Most injected NPs are captured by the reticuloendothelial system (RES), which is abundant in the liver and spleen [5]. The liver is the largest digestive organ in the body and serves as a surveillance system of foreign substances in the blood circulation. The current delivery strategies are to prevent internalization by RES and to improve circulation time [4]. Besides the RES, other liver cells also have the capacity to interact with NPs [6]. These hepatic cells work together to achieve immune surveillance in the liver [7] ( Fig. 1). Many diseases are associated with alterations in the liver, such as changes in the availability of bioactive peptides, enzymes, or proteins secreted by hepatocytes. Therefore, the liver becomes the optimal target organ for nanotherapeutic applications. Under diseased conditions, some surface markers in liver cells are altered and may no longer respond to the targeting strategy. Therefore, we explored targeted delivery strategies in both the healthy and diseased liver, to clarify the relationships between NPs and liver cells. Regulating the intrinsic properties of NPs can significantly change their distribution in vivo. In this review, we describe the potential targeting strategies for liver disease treatment by understanding the interactions between NPs and liver cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanoparticles are highly accumulated in the liver
Blood supply and the hepatic RES promote the accumulation of nanoparticles in the liver
ce:section.ce:para: Systemic administration of nonmodified NPs results in preferential transport to the liver rather than to other organs. This is largely due to the anatomy and blood supply of the liver. The unique blood supply of the liver can be divided into arterial and venous veins. One-third of the oxygen-rich arterial blood mixes with the nutrient-rich venous blood from the intestinal tract and spleen in the portal vein and supplies the blood circulation of the liver. The ideal position makes the liver a desired organ to capture pathogens through the gut and eliminate bacteria, viruses, and NPs from the blood circulation. Blood flows into the liver via the portal vein, passing through several hepatic sinusoids to the central vein. There are many resident phagocytes in the sinusoids, which screen and filter foreign materials in the blood. Thus, the liver has the capacity to eliminate oral NPs derived from the gut and systemic NPs injected into the peripheral blood circulation.

Most of the injected NPs are cleared by the RES. The largest population of macrophages in the body are liver-resident Kupffer cells (KCs). KCs express many kinds of immune receptors and scavenger receptors, which may facilitate receptor-mediated endocytosis. KCs can be identified by CLEC4F in mouse but are not conserved in humans. Furthermore, CLEC4F is expressed only in the mature KCs and not in the developing KCs. Guilliams et al. concluded that CD5L, VSIG4, CD163, FOLR2, MARCO, and SLC40A1 can be used to identify KC in the liver in seven species [8]. In addition, other types of macrophages have been described in the liver. Liver capsule macrophages (LCM) are derived from monocytes and accumulate in the liver capsule [9]. They express F4/80 and CD64 but not KC markers, such as CLEC4F, CLEC2, VSIG4 or FLOR2. Furthermore, the transcriptional signature of KCs is shared by hepatic macrophages present in the central vein [8]. Similarly, other hepatic macrophages, present in the bile duct of the healthy liver and fatty liver, are defined as lipid-associated macrophages (LAM) [10,11]. KCs can also be replenished by circulated monocytes, according to the integrated niche in the liver [8,12]. Monocyte-derived KCs (moKCs) express markers are also identified on KC and gain full functions of KCs under the regulation of the microenvironmental niche.

Liver sinusoidal endothelial cells (LSECs) represent the other population of RES in the liver. Direct interaction with NPs in the blood circulation allows targeted and non-targeted internalization to occur. When blood flows into the liver, the flow rate will change dramatically. It is 1000-fold slower in the liver sinusoid [13], which leads to the prolonged accumulation of substances. NPs generally adhere to the vascular wall when they enter the sinusoids of the liver. The LSECs are connected with fenestrae (also consider as discontinuous endothelium), which permit the freely distribution of NPs, <200 nm in size, to the space of Disse. Although not professional phagocytes, the HSCs and hepatocytes have the capacity to clear and digest NPs in the liver space of Disse. Therefore, NPs with small size (6–200 nm) are more likely to be accumulated in the liver organ. Under these conditions, the liver becomes the largest organ for the accumulation of NPs. Therefore, recognizing the interaction of NPs with liver cells supports the optimal strategy for nanotherapies in liver diseases.

Oral administration is the most suitable modality for patients. The gut and liver have a unique relationship in metabolic, neuroendocrine, and immune interactions, since the sinusoids of the liver and the gastrointestinal tract are connected by the portal and biliary circulation [14,15]. Orally administered NPs have the tendency to be transported further into the portal vein. Nonetheless, NPs must face the challenges present in the gastrointestinal tract [16]. The columnar epithelium forms the core layer of the gut barrier that blocks the transportation of NPs into the portal vein due to the presence of tight junctions between the intestinal epithelial cells. Below this layer, the largest population of mononuclear macrophages and DCs eliminates foreign substances from the blood. Subsequently, the lymph associated with the intestinal tract is drained to the mesenteric lymph nodes, which restricts the spread of lymphogenesis and maintains the homeostatic status of the liver. These interactions can be described as the gut-liver axis, highlighting the relevant link between gut and liver diseases.

Intravenous and intraperitoneal injections are also commonly adopted because these drugs cannot be absorbed by the gastrointestinal tract or by muscle tissue. Both routes can overcome the degradation of various enzymes in metabolically active organs. In addition, intravenous administration offers control over the injection speed. Systemic administration can directly transport NPs to the blood circulation, resulting in rapid onset of action and a full utilization rate. It is inevitable that NPs are transported to the liver given its abundant blood supply.

Other routes of administration can also be used for the delivery of NPs but have failed in the in practicality, avoidance of pain, or controlled release. The inhalation route has irreplaceable benefits on pulmonary drug delivery, with the major limitation of NP treatment being that clinical inhalers are not suitable for the inhalation of NPs. Retinal delivery, such as subretinal, intravitreal, or suprachoroidal administration, is completely dependent on the presence of a specialized surgeon. Interestingly, intranasal administration with cationic liposomes has modified mRNA expression in the brain [17]. However, this route still needs to be improved as off-target accumulation accumulates in the trachea and lungs. Therefore, oral and intravenous delivery are the most convenient, bioavailable, and biocompatible routes for NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interactions of NPs with hepatic cells in the liver diseases
ce:section.ce:para: In the homeostasis of the liver, the mechanisms of liver resident cells ensure the presence of a tolerant microenvironment. T cells are responsible for immune suppression under the control of quiescent HSCs, KCs, LSECs, and hepatocytes. MHC II and costimulatory molecules are also expressed at a low level in nonimmune cells of the liver, suggesting the maintenance of the liver immune tolerant microenvironment [18]. In the diseased liver, most cells switch to activation modes and immune responses are rapidly activated. During the inflammatory microenvironment, cytokines can be secreted, and neutrophils, monocytes, natural killer (NK) cells, and NK T cells (NKT) are recruited. The reduction or even loss of the resident KC population has been observed in liver diseases such as NAFLD [19]. The phenotypes of LSECs are self-modified and have a negative regulation on the other neighboring hepatic cells during liver pathophysiology [20]. It is necessary to fully understand the populations and phenotypes changes in diseased liver for the precise delivery of NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immune cells directly govern the liver immune microenvironment
ce:section.ce:para: Hepatic macrophages are the major regulatory cells with remarkable influence on immune microenvironment. KCs maintain the homeostasis of the liver microenvironment, serving as not only gate keepers, but can also secret cytokines and chemokines. KCs also play an important role during the progression of many liver diseases. As professional phagocytic cells, it is quite simple for to exploit KCs in targeted delivery strategies. However, the differences in cellular microenvironment, low pH level, and lysosome size make it much harder for NPs to escape or alter gene expression. High mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) is highly expressed in mouse NASH models and aggravate liver damage. A mannose-modified lipid nanoparticles (LNP) coated HMGB1-siRNA were used as a Kupffer cell nucleic acid delivery system. Through the mannose receptors, the HMGB1 gene was effectively silenced in liver KCs [21]. Under normal conditions, liver KCs can be divided into embryo-derived Kupffer cells (EmKCs) and monocyte-derived KCs (MoKCs). Approximately 90% of KCs are self-renewed from EmKCs. In chronic liver diseases, monocytes are recruited to form a pool of KCs. These macrophages are usually defined as pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages instead of immune-suppressed M2 type macrophages. Scavenger receptor-A (SR-A) expressed on the surface of M1 macrophages becomes an optimized target site for drug delivery in acute liver injury [22]. Pedro et al., designed graphene nanostars, which linked the PAMAM-G5 dendrimer for liver macrophages-targeted delivery. The metalloproteinase 9 plasmid was loaded into the graphene nanostars, and overexpression caused macrophage phenotypes to change from M1 to M2 in 3 days [23].

NK cells occupy one-third of the population of lymphocytes in liver. In the healthy liver, resident NK cells have limited qualification and patrol in liver sinusoids; but are quickly activated in an inflammatory liver, especially in viral infection by KC [24]. NK cells demonstrated the potential to inhibit liver metastasis of colorectal cancer (CRC) and served as targeted cells for IL-12 [25] or a combination of seleninic acid, LY345899, and cetuximab by nanoparticles [26]. Invariant natural killer T cells (iNKT) are a specialized subset of T cells that express αβ TCR by selection in the thymus. iNKT cells are recruited to liver sinusoids by their surface CXCR6 to CXCL16 secreted from LSEC and KC [27]. αGalCer/CD1d-coated nanoparticles triggered liver iNKT in immunoregulatory cells, producing IL-10 or IL-21, and suppressing autoantigen-induced autoimmunity [28].

Dendritic cells (DCs) usually remain in the area of the portal tracts as immature cells in the healthy liver, and form periportal lymphoid structures under inflammation [29]. Liver DCs have limited immunogenic functions but can be activated via TLR and RAGE receptors [30]. R848 (TLR agonist), stattic (STAT 3 inhibitor) and DHA-conjugated antigen delivered by SVMAV Nanovaccine achieved better antitumor activity than the PD-1 antibody in mouse orthotopic hepatocellular cancer [31].

Neutrophils are considered the main contributors to liver inflammation in acute liver inflammatory, infectious diseases, liver surgery, and chronic liver diseases. Neutrophils should be considered as an essential factor for NPs delivery during these diseases. They serve as potential targeted cells or main barriers for the accumulations of NPs in other organs [32,33]. The inflammatory response also benefits treatment in the tumor microenvironment. Increased neutrophil production in the liver provides an opportunity for neutrophil membrane-coated nanoparticles (NNPs) to inhibit tumor progression and liver metastases [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nonimmune cells also contribute to the maintenance of the immune microenvironment of the liver
ce:section.ce:para: LSECs are the main non-parenchyma cell of the liver, occupies a ratio of more than 50%. Meanwhile, LSECs have the ability to present antigens, a critical step for liver immune surveillance. The low expression of costimulatory molecules in LSECs leads to immune tolerance of the liver. LSEC induces CD4 and CD8 T cells in the liver to suppress the immune phenotype and also interacts with DC to regulate T cells indirectly [18]. Stable status means that LSECs regulate immune responses without being transported to lymph nodes. This rapid response allows it to act as a suitable site for inducing immune tolerance.

Many studies have focused on LSECs targeted delivery, for the regulation of immune environment. The internalization of NPs meditated by the mannose receptor also works on the surface of LSECs. The ideal target site is conducive to the delivery of antigens to LSECs by liposomes. Decorated mRNA induces the production of IL-10 and TGF-β in the peritoneum, and finally alleviates the potential for anaphylaxis to food allergens [35]. With the burden of tumor in the liver, the tumor-associated immune suppressed macroenvironment further inhibits the expression of costimulatory molecules and MHC-II expression. α-melittin-NP can target the activation of the immune modulation of LSECs on the antitumor effects of NK cells and T cells [36] ( Fig. 3).

Hepatic stellate cell (HSC) is another non-parenchymal cell type found in liver sinusoids, representing ∼10% of resident hepatic cells. HSC is the major site for vitamin A storage, which is vital for the synthesis of many types of immune cytokines and chemokines, such as IL-17 and CCL2. HSC has the potential to be activated in fibroblast and secretes collagen and extracellular matrix [37]. Therefore, the progression of liver fibrosis is closely dependent on HSC [38,39]. Liver fibrosis can be induced by many factors such as virus infection, alcoholic steatohepatitis (ASH), nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) and other liver damage. How to reverse activated HSC to quiescence is a key strategy in the treatment of liver fibrosis. HSC has limited cell quantities distributed in the liver sinusoids. Furthermore, poor phagocytosis and antigen capture ability contribute to the reduced capacity for HSC NP uptake. To improve nanodrug delivery to HSC, optimization of the nanoplatform and strategy is indispensable.

Under conditions of fibrosis, the extracellular matrix consists mainly of collagen fibers. The fenestrae in the liver sinusoids are markedly reduced during the progression of fibrosis, leading to the formation of additional barriers for the delivery of NPs. Combination therapy is often used to overcome these complicated problems. Initially, simvastatin or riociguat was used to rebuild the fenestrae in sinusoids [40]. This repair allows NPs to enter the space of Disse, relying on the insulin growth factor 2 receptor-mediated HSCs-targeted delivery of IGNP-JQ1 for anti-fibrosis treatment [40] ( Fig. 4). Hence, the second barrier can be overcome via collagenase to ablate collagen or targeted decorations of HSC [40,41]. The strategy of restoring LSECs fenestrae may contribute to the transport of NPs into the space of Disse in chronic liver diseases. A hallmark of activated HSC (aHSC), integrin αvβ3, can be targeted by the cRGDyK-liposome, and promotes targeted delivery [42]. HSC (LX2 cells) membrane-coated PLGA NPs provide homologous targeting to HSCs in the treatment of liver fibrosis [43].

Hepatocytes occupy approximately 70% of liver mass [44], and are essential for the performance of liver functions. The liver is the main digestive organ, whose activity is dependent on multiple enzymes produced by hepatocytes. Most genetic disorders of the liver are associated with inborn errors in hepatocytes. The surface markers are usually expressed but there are no distinctive transformed phenotypes. In chronic diseases such as fibrosis or HCC, dramatic variations are observed. Diverse strategies will be adopted to assess the status of hepatocyte discrepancies.

The most common LNP strategy for the delivery of hepatocytes are GalNAc-ASGPR and ApoE-HSPG-LDLR [45]. GalNAc can be not only decorated on the surface of NPs but also directly linked to the end of siRNA for targeted delivery. Adjusting the ratio of different formulations in LNPs can finally determine their accumulation in hepatocytes. In an mRNA delivery system, decreasing the DOTAP ratio benefits hepatocyte uptake; replacing DOTAP with DODAP also enhances liver accumulation [46]. The first approved siRNA drug Onpattro™ is administered to hepatocytes by LNPs using the apoE strategy, for the treatment of polyneuropathies induced by hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis [47]. Furthermore, this LNPs vector has also been adopted for mRNA delivery in clinical trials [48]. Typically, a LNP contains an ionizable lipid head such as C12–200 [49,50], dimethyldioctadecylammonium (DDA) and 1,2-dioleoyl-3-trimethylammonium propane (DOTAP) [51], a helper lipid such as cholesterol and phospholipid; and a PEGylated shell covered on the surface.

Similarly, collagenase can be decorated with nanodelivery systems, which benefit the circulations of NPs in liver diseases. Liver fibrosis eventually progresses to HCC, and cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAF) serve as key agents for targeted delivery. IGFRII or PDGFRβ on HSC/CAF can be an effectively directed drug delivery in liver cancer nanotherapies [52]. CAFs are also a potential target point for vitamin B3 reprograming and stimulation of robust antitumor effects of T cells [53]. Another obvious signal of diseased hepatocytes is CD44, and its increased expression offers a target site for HCC treatments. The anticancer drug 5-fluorouracil (5-Fu) or anti-miR-27a can be transported to HCC cells using the CD44 target strategy by nanodelivery systems [54,55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characteristics of NPs determine their biodistribution in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The characteristics of nanoparticles have a great impact on their fate in vivo. Size, shape, surface charge and surface modifications can affect the interactions between nanoparticles and bioactive proteins. Nanoparticles experience shear stress, when entering the blood flow [87], and some organs and cells constitute the clearing effect on NPs [88]. After systemic injection, NPs encounter many kinds of bioactive proteins in the bloodstream, such as immunoglobulin, complement, apolipoprotein and albumin, and they form together as the protein corona. The formulations of the protein corona are changeable during the circulation of NPs. Some of them have a dissociation rate and can be easily exchanged and are called the soft corona; while the others with a low dissociation rate remain stable and are called the hard corona. Most formulations of protein corona consist of apolipoproteins (ApoB, ApoE) or lipoproteins (LDL, VLDL), and their specific receptor are expressed on liver hepatocytes. This may help internalize NPs by hepatocytes. The speed of blood flow is changeable in different organs, when the blood flow changes, the shear stress on NPs also varies. This may have an influence on the surface constructure of NPs. Different materials have different resistances to the shear force depending on their size, stiffiness, and shape. Surface modification such as PEGylated prevent cellular uptake; antibodies, ligands or small molecular drugs on NPs may facilitate endocytic ( Fig. 5). The physical properties of NPs are the major factors that determine their biodistribution in vivo. And the function of NPs is strongly associated with their biodistribution.

Inorganic NPs (gold, silica, silver, titanium dioxide, zinc oxide, and iron oxide) usually stimulate the immune response, while organic NPs (LNPs and PLGA polymer) merely induce that. The immunogenicity of NPs depends on their physicochemical properties and the immune system of the host. The size effect induces distinctive immune responses with different immune cells. NK cells’ activation was induced by the 12 nm gold NPs, while 2 nm gold NPs were more freely taken up by immune cells without the production of proinflammatory cytokines [89]. Similarly, 100 nm zinc oxide NPs showed more lactate dehydrogenase release in NK cells compared to 20 nm [90]. However, if we chose DCs as the target cells, small gold NPs (< 10 nm) activated the NLRP3 inflammasome, while the larger size NPs (>10 nm) triggered the NF-κB signaling pathway [91]. Surface charge will also determine the degree of immune responses. The same sized zinc oxide NPs with negative charge led to stronger immune system activation than positive charged NPs [90]. The immune response induced by NPs may turn to activation as a vaccine adjuvant or suppression for immune tolerance. Different immune responses will be determined by different types of NPs. Moreover, immune cells can react differently in a size and charge-dependent manner of the certain types of NPs.

Size is the fundamental factor in the passage of NPs across physical or biological barriers, such as the blood-brain barrier (BBB). Among all types of endothelial cells, hepatic sinusoid, and kidney endothelial cells have fenestra and allow substances to pass. The distance between glomerular endothelial cells (GECs) is about 6 nm. GECs participate in the sieving properties of the glomerular filtration barrier and in the maintenance of podocyte structure [92]. The LSECs are highly fenestrated, and the distance between them can be varied, ranging from a few to hundreds of nanometers. Smaller NPs are allowed to be transported to HSCs and hepatocytes through the fenestrae of LSECs.

Usually, when the size of NP is less than 5.5 nm, it can easily cross the GEC barrier and excrete through the kidney [93]. This elimination process is very quick; only a few hours are needed. Many strategies have been attempted for the renal elimination of NPs, the primary methods are the adjustment of size, density of the core, surface charge, and surface modification of NPs [94,95]. The size range of the NPs from 5.5 to 200 nm can be transported from the fenestrae of the LSEC, allowing further access to hepatocytes, and are finally excreted by the feces.

Most nanoparticles are spheres, rods, cubes, and nanocages. In the bloodstream, the nanoparticles are under a flowing environment, which induces fluid forces. Different shapes of nanoparticles cause different edge margination. Simply, rods and ellipsoids with higher aspect ratios are more likely to be located in blood vessels than in spheres. However, if they lack endothelial cell-specific uptake, these nanoparticles near vessels may be stripped by the blood flow. Therefore, the shape of NPs should be considered to be a major influence of their biodistribution in vascular pathologies such as hypertension and atherosis. Furthermore, the uptake by the cell membrane and nanoparticles are related to the contact area [96]. Spheres are more likely to be internalized to nonphagocytes than rods [97,98].

The surface charge is another important characteristic of NPs, and is a major influence on the biodistribution in vivo. First, the surface charge can directly determine the types of protein corona. NPs with high positive charge have stable attraction with negative charge proteins, and the difference in potential may lead to changes in the corona. The fate of nanoparticles can be greatly affected by the protein corona. For example, albumin or ApoE has a specific receptor on the surface of liver hepatocytes, which cloud causes the uptake of gold nanoparticles by hepatocytes and is finally excreted by the feces [99]. Interestingly, albumin coated 15 nm Au NPs showed higher accumulation in lung and brain compared to ApoE covered Au NPs; while such phenomenon disappeared when the size increased to 80 nm. These data indicated that the fate of nanoparticles is determined by complex factors. The physical characteristic of NPs still occupied the dominance of the biodistribution of nanoparticles. Phagocytosis of macrophages can influence different types of corona proteins. Clusterin (ApoJ) in the protein corona reduces nonspecific cellular uptake [100], while immunoglobulins and complements increase macrophage internalization [101].

Surface modification aims to increase/avoid reactions between NPs and cells. To avoid the rapid elimination caused by the surface characteristics of NPs, PEG is usually used as the modification. PEGylated vitalizes the hydrophobicity of NPs, which prevents cellular internalization. The half-life of NPs in the blood circulation will be significantly enhanced after PEGylation. This modification also blocks the reactions between the surface of NPs and the antibodies or enzymes of the organism. Modification of an antibody, peptide, or small molecule can also change the fate of NPs in the blood circulation. CD47 can interact with the SIPR-alpha-expressing macrophage and act as a 'don't eat me’ signal to suppress macrophage phagocytosis [102]. Nanoparticles coated with biomimetic red blood cell membranes (RBC) rely on CD47 on the surface to prolong the circulatory time and reduce the speed of blood clearance [103]. Targeted delivery also relies on the surface modifications on NPs. The internalization of most NPs is mediated by receptor-dependent endocytosis. HER2, a member of the human epidermal growth factor receptor family, plays a crucial role in breast cancer. Trastuzumab works as a humanized anti-HER2 antibody, assists nanoparticles to be transported to HER2 positive tumor cells, and activates the cytotoxicity response [104].

Physical properties of NPs play a dominant role on their biodistributions. Those properties mentioned above will be no doubts affect the fate of NPs. But, the biodegradability of NPs should also be carefully considered. The delivery goals require that NPs should be transported to the targeted tissue and targeted cells. Therefore, most NPs are stable in the blood circulation after injections. When they traveled to the diseased organs, drugs will be released to the target cells. Parts of degradation or off-self-assembly products of nanocarriers may be eliminated out of body, while the others may resident in the organs for a long time.

Therefore, the retention of nanocarriers or nanomaterials also raise the concerns on adverse effects. Simply, most of organic NPs are biodegradable, such as LNPs and PLGA polymer. The components of these NPs are usually harmless to the organisms, as they are carefully selected in the early design. Inorganic NPs, especially the metal materials, tended to be resident in the body for years, which causes the risks of adverse effects in the long term. However, for the patients with advanced tumors, a three-month prolonged survival would be meaningful. The possible of accompanied adverse effects seem to be acceptable.

The biological fate of inorganic NPs is crucial for their uses in drug delivery and biomedical applications. The biodegradation and dissolution products of inorganic NPs may lead to long term accumulation and induce immune responses [105]. In the past decades, we consider the immunogenicity equal to the immunotoxicity. But now, more and more applications are depended on the modulations to immune system by nanomaterials. The aluminum NPs have been successfully utilized as pulmonary vaccine adjuvants as their immune activations on antigen presenting cells [106]. There is a promising future for the applications on the immunoregulation by nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications based on nanoparticle delivery system for the treatment of liver disease

ce:section.ce:para: Liver disease causes about 2 million deaths annually and accounts for 4% of total deaths [107]. Most deaths are associated with the progression of liver cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). HCC is the fourth tumor-associated death worldwide. Many factors such as the hepatitis B and C virus, alcohol abuse, and nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) will induce chronic cirrhosis and ultimately develop into HCC [108]. Furthermore, with the obesity pandemic, there is increasing concern about the serious challenge of NAFLD-related HCC [109].

More than 80% of primary liver tumors are defined by HCC. As a result of the lack of effective therapeutics, the 5-year survival of HCC is only 20%. Liver transplantation will greatly prolong HCC survival, but the lack of donor organs and recrudescence after transplantation also limit this therapy. Patients who cannot tolerate surgery or chemotherapeutics generally receive systemic therapies, targeted therapy, and immune therapy [110]. Commonly used first-line agents for HCC are Sorafenib and Lenvatinib, and their resistance limits their beneficial effects. Thus, second-line targeted agents areurgently needed for HCC treatment. Treatment with Regorafenib, Cabozantinib, and Ramucirumab as second-line agents has been evaluated in clinical trials. However, not all patients who progressed in Sorafenib are suitable for Regorafenib. Cabozantinib has the risk of high-grade adverse events [111]. Combination with immune therapy can effectively enhance treatment, but improvements usually occur a few months after treatment. Additionally, the response rate is not optimistic for most patients [112].

To improve antitumor treatment, there is a growing demand for NP drug delivery systems to hepatocytes. The infection of Hepatitis virus and cirrhosis (induced by NASH or diabetes mellitus) in hepatocytes are considered as the high risks for HCC. Recently, many types of nanoplatforms have been attempted in HCC treatment. Inorganic metal NPs such as gold, silver, and platinum; Inorganic non-metal NPs include carbon and silicon; organic NPs contain liposomes, polymers, and hydrogel. NPs can be decorated with targeted modifications to HCC. Chemotherapeutic drugs, doxorubicin or paclitaxel, and targeted drugs have been enveloped in liposomes as cancer therapies with the aim of targeted delivery and reducing side effects [113,114]. With decorations on NPs, the circulation time is profoundly prolonged and the accumulation of these drugs in hepatocytes is also enhanced. Doxorubicin liposomes have been approved for clinical treatment. Similarly, the delivery vehicles have the potential to induce cell death in tumors. Targeted delivery is particularly necessary, as the killing effect may also occur in normal tissues. DOX-liposomes with CD44 targeted strategy not only accumulated 7-fold more in HCC tumor, but showed less accumulation in other normal tissues compared to free DOX drug [115]. The overexpressing of CD44 on HCC is an attractive therapeutic target for nanoparticles [54,55]. A NaBH4 peptide modified Pt NPs can directly target HCC and release Pt2+ which induces apoptosis [116]. mPEG-PLGA-PLL-decorated As2O3 provided better HCC tumor inhibition by upregulating GSDME-N expression compared to As2O3 alone [117]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have supramolecular size, favorable hydrophilia, and excellent photoacoustics, which have become a smart vector in tumor diagnosis and treatment [118,119]. CQDs can also be modified by multiple functional groups for various purposes [120]. Trichrome-tryptophan-sorbitol CQDs (TC-WS-CQDs) are highly sensitive to green fluorescence and mark HCC cells for targeted delivery. Furthermore, TC-WS-CQD also suppressed tumor growth by inducing autophagy [121]. ZnS@BSA nanoclusters enhance the cCAS/STING pathway and promote the activation of CD8 T cells in the tumor microenvironments [122].

Infectious diseases of the liver still plague healthy global health. Worldwide, more than 296 million people are infected with HBV, and 331,000 deaths have been estimated in HBV-related liver disorders [123]. HBV is a hepatotropic virus with the potential to persist in hepatocytes and causes cirrhosis and HCC without timely treatment. Current treatments include pegylated interferon-α and nucleoside analogues (NA) [124,125]. The purpose of treatment is to avoid progression to liver cirrhosis, failure, and HCC [126]. Besides, the novel antisense oligonucleotide, bepirovirsen, that targets all HBV messenger RNAs and acts to decrease the levels of viral proteins (HBsAg) quickly after treatment in a phase 2b trial (NCT04449029) [127]. Patients with or without NA treatment both showed efficacy and safety to bepirovirsen, however, the recovery of HBsAg levels were observed at reduced dose or at the end of the treatment [127]. These available antiviral therapies do not clear covalently closed circular DNA (cccDNA), which is the only limit in virus replication. The biggest barrier to cure HBV is the removal of cccDNA. A novel gene editing tool, Cas9, makes it possible to cure HBV infection. Many research efforts have aimed to deliver the Cas9 system using nanoplatforms for the depletion of cccDNA [128–130]. With the guide of gRNA, the Cas9 protein or ribonucleoprotein (RNP) can target cccDNA and initiate gene editing. NP drug delivery systems also contributed to antiviral treatments, such as preventing cell infection, disrupting viral DNA or RNA replication, and co-delivery as an immunopotentiator [131–133].

The liver is not a classical secondary lymphoid organ in the body. The microenvironment of the liver is mainly determined by liver-residing cells (KCs, LSECs, DCs, and hepatocytes), as most are involved in functionally suppressing the adaptive immune response. The DCs, the main professional antigen presenting cells (APCs) in the liver, have low expression of CD11c and class II MHC and secrete anti-inflammatory cytokines IL-10. Similarly, KCs also lack the expression of costimulatory molecules and MHC class II. The absence of costimulatory molecules and the production of PGE2 and IL-10 inhibit T cell proliferation and antigen-specific T cell activation [134–136]. LSECs work as true APCs with expression of MHC I and II, only without migration to lymph nodes. In the presence of PD-L1 expression, cross-presenting suppresses the immune response. Although the primary functions of hepatocytes are metabolism, toxin neutralization, and protein production, they can also serve as APCs under some conditions. There are costimulatory molecules and class I MHC expressed in hepatocytes and in an inflammatory environment, and class II MHC can also be detected on the surface of cells. In the presence of IL-10 cytokines, hepatocytes and LSEC contribute to liver immune hyporesponsiveness [137,138].

Liver transplant tolerance does not demand immunosuppressive therapy in animals, as humans, donor and recipient histocompatibility are not considered a serious factor as in kidney transplantations for the survival of graft. Carefully selected patients have the possibility to completely avoid immunosuppressive treatment [139]. The immune tolerant microenvironment of the liver reduces the risks of rejection.

However, donor organs are in extreme shortage, which causes an increased ratio of non-ideal match between donor and recipient. Organs often come from donors with poor physical conditions and lack of sufficient blood and nutrient supply. Some organs from deceased donors are often declined due to perceived and actual suboptimal quality [140]. There are dramatic difficulties in donor treatment. Therefore, protection of the grafts from transport to transplantation is of vital importance. Ischemia/reperfusion injury (IRI) occurs due to a lack of organ blood flow followed the restoration of its perfusion and is the main challenge in organ transplantation [141]. The liver is one of the most abundant organs supplied with blood flow and may suffer serious damage during the restoration of perfusion. Liver sinusoids are consisted of KCs and LSECs, these cells are under more pressure on reperfusion. To reduce the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), Ceria reductive NPs have been injected to suppress the inflammatory reaction in the liver [142]. Polymorphonuclear neutrophil (PMN)-induced IRI injury can be treated with NPs enveloped with NOD1 antagonist NPs [143]. The activation of APCs in the liver can be regulated by liposomes loaded with TLR-4 siRNA for hepatocyte-targeted delivery in the prevention of IRI [144].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted delivery for autoimmune diseases treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Autoimmune diseases in the liver often occur when immune tolerance is lost. Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) is a T-cell-related immune response to liver autoantigens. Immunosuppressive treatment provides great benefits in the early stage of the disease. Immune tolerance can be reinduced by specific autoantigenic epitopes delivery to liver APCs [35,83,85] and re-program T cells, as shown in Fig. 8. Primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) or autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) associated peptide-major histocompatibility complex class II (pMHCII) coated on PEGylated iron oxide NPs can broadly recruit B regulatory (B reg) cells to suppress liver APCs’ activation on T cells [145]. Furthermore, pMHC nanomedicines have allowed the restoration of T regulatory (T reg) cells, which contribute to T cell suppression [146]. Standard treatment of patients is a high dose of corticosteroids (prednisolone or dexamethasone, Dex). Adverse reactions are usually induced by long-term use of corticosteroids. NPs offer the potential to improve the circulation times of loaded drugs and significantly reduce the dose administered. A biodegradable Avidin-Nucleic-Acid-Nano-Assemblies (ANANAS) carrier linked to Dex reduced the inflammatory response in AIH progression [147]. PLA polymer NPs encapsulated with proteolipid protein (PLP139–151) revealed the inhibition of T cell polarization to Th1 and Th17 in multiple sclerosis (MS) [148].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted delivery for liver fibrosis treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Liver fibrosis is a usually consequence of chronic inflammation and is a common result of viral, metabolic, toxic and autoimmune diseases. The hepatic immune cells maintain tolerance under non-inflammatory conditions and quickly regulate other liver immune cells or non-parenchymal cells activation under injury. The activation of HSCs is the main contributor of ECM and α-SMA production. As we mentioned above in Fig. 4, HSCs targeted delivery treatment showed effective antifibrotic strategy with combination therapy. The consideration of the crosstalk between other cells with HSCs illustrated remarkable therapeutic effects on liver fibrosis. Similarly, melatonin was used to ameliorate liver fibrosis via two biomimetic NPs for hepatocytes and HSCs modification. Platelet membranes (PM) and activated hepatic stellate cell membranes (HSCM) were selected to construct PM@PLGA@Melatonin (hepatocytes targeting) and HSCM@PLGA@Melatonin (HSCs targeting) to treat mice with liver fibrosis [149]. The restore of multicellular crosstalk on LSECs, HSCs, and hepatocytes provided a promising antifibrotic effect. Briefly, chondroitin sulfate-modified and vismodegib-loaded nanoparticles (CS-NPs/VDG) are designed to normalize the fenestrae of LSECs and restore HSCs to quiescent by inhibiting Hedgehog signaling pathway. Meanwhile, glycyrrhetinic acid-modified and silybin-loaded nanoparticles (GA-NPs/SIB) repaired the oxidative stress in hepatocytes [150]. Therefore, targeted delivery provides more opportunities for various hepatic cells modulation rather than single activated HSCs inhibition. The combination strategy with different NPs prompts novel insights for the antifibrotic treatment in the future.

Gene therapies have been approved in many clinical trials and provide positive responses. However, the greatest challenge of gene therapies is the demand for ideal transport vehicles, which is mainly attributed to the existence of a variety of enzymes in the organism that can digest foreign nuclear acids. DNases and RNases could be found in extracellular, cytoplasmic, and nuclear. DNA can activate TLR4 and RNA will activate TLR7/8/9 in macrophages or DCs and induce innate immune responses. The nuclear acid cargos of gene therapies include DNA, siRNA, threptic proteins of mRNA, and gene editing tools (Cas9 protein or Cas9 mRNA and sgRNA) [151]. siRNA is usually delivered to match the target gene mRNA and interrupt the translation to proteins. Plasmid DNA and mRNA are commonly used for the expression of a desired gene. The powerful editing tools can be classified as Meganucleases, transcription activator-like effector nucleases, zinc-finger nucleases (ZFN), and CRISPR/CRISPR-associated 9 (CRISPR-Cas9). CRISPR-Cas9 has the ability to easily and precisely engineer genomic DNA and has become widely popular in recent years [152]. The growing development of mRNA drugs is mainly dependent on LNP vectors. LNPs are the ideal transport carriers and are used to avoid off-target delivery and reduce immunogenicity. To enhance gene editing efficacy, Cas9 is loaded with a RNP formed by LNPs [153,154].

More than 75,000 types of diseases have been found to be associated with genetic mutations. These heritable traits and diseases are usually caused by monogenic or polygenic changes in the genome and are apparently limited to the specificity of tissue or cell types in the body [155]. Recent technological advances have allowed precision treatment to move to the clinic [156]. For some hepatic metabolic disorders caused by lost expression of key enzymes, particular mRNA delivery will greatly relieve disease progression. Such diseases include acute intermittent porphyria (AIP), methymalonic acidemia (MMA), Fabry disease, citrin deficiency, type 3 familial progressive intrahepatic cholestasis (PFIC3) and so on. Meanwhile, inherited nonhepatic diseases also can be cured with hepatocyte-targeted drug delivery. Therapeutic proteins can reproduce in the system circulation from the liver. Hemophilia A and B are caused by the deficiency of coagulation factors VIII (FVIII) and IX (FIX) and could be cured by Cas9 in vivo gene editing by LNPs [157]. Alpha 1 antitrypsin deficiency disease (AATD) causes lung emphysema due to the lack of the AAT protein. AAT mRNA-loaded Mtx-LNPs have been reported to successfully recover AAT levels in animal models [158]. A series of LNP vectors has been introduced into clinic trials by the pharmaceutical company Moderna. A Phase I/II study (NCT04899310) on MMA treatment with Mtx LNP-hMUT mRNA (mRNA-3704) is underway. Patients with GSD1a (NCT05095727) are being treated with Mtx LNP-hG6Pase mRNA (mRNA-3745), whereas participants in the NCT0415910 trial are being injected with Mtx LNP-hPCCA/B mRNA (mRNA 3927) [159]. Genome editing seems to be a potential cure for patients requiring life-long drug treatment. The success of the LNP vector in ATTR disease in Phase I trials ( Fig. 9) [48] will encourage more treatment attempts in the future.

The structural and blood supply properties of the liver are responsible for the accumulation of NPs. Oral and systemic administration of NPs are the most acceptable routes for most patients. Such routes promote the accumulation of NPs in the liver. Understanding the relevance between liver and NPs will dramatically enhance the efficacy of drug delivery to liver cells including liver resident KCs, DCs, NK, NKT, and memory T cells; in addition, the liver has the potential to recruit monocytes and neutrophils. The role of these immune cells is to remove foreign substances from the hepatic blood circulation. Non-immune cells, hepatocytes, cholangiocytes, HSCs, and LSECs also maintain the homeostasis of liver immunology as the main sensors and triggers of immune responses. TLR4 is expressed by hepatocytes, LSECs and HSCs, activating the immune system. Thus, the interactions between hepatic cells and NPs should be considered during the design of targeted delivery NPs platforms, particularly in the diseased liver.

As mentioned above, different nanomaterials have different fates in the in vivo circulation. We focused on the adjustment of the self-properties of NPs, on the optimization of the size, shape, surface charge, and surface modifications. These basic factors determine the response of NPs to blood shear stress, the formulations of protein corona during blood circulation, and the uptake mediated by specific receptors by particular cell types. Physical properties such as size and shape are the easiest ways to change the biodistribution of NPs in vivo. The surface charge is most relative to the compounds of the protein corona, as the apolipoprotein (ApoB, ApoE) or lipoprotein (LDL, VLDL) may be coated at the surface of NPs by electrostatic attraction. PEGylation provides a shell of NPs that prevents cell-to-cell reactions with the cell membrane, enhancing the circulation time of most nanodelivery systems. Modifications to antibodies, ligands, or functional groups often offer a unique interaction between NPs and cells. Such methods are the most effective strategies for targeted nanodelivery system.

Liver cells can be considered as targeted cells in various liver diseases treatments. The liver can be dismissed as an immune organ, which induces immune tolerance under homeostasis conditions. However, marked changes have been found in hepatic cells during diseased liver. The altered phenotypes of hepatic cells reveal a novel target point for NPs. Such strategies may enhance the therapeutic effect in the diseased liver. Recognizing the interactions between NPs and hepatic cells in homeostasis and diseased conditions will profoundly improve the performance of nanotherapeutics on immune activation or induction of immune tolerance. The targeting of hepatocytes remains a challenge for most nanoplatforms. The blood-hepatic sinusoid barrier prevents the accumulation of NPs in hepatocytes. Nuclear acid drugs or gene editing tools desperately need suitable transport vehicles. NPs reveal many advantages in gene therapies for liver diseases.

In conclusion, the principles learned from the interactions of NPs and liver cells distinctly guide the design of nanodelivery systems for different purposes in both the homeostatic and diseased liver. Optimal designs of NPs for different liver diseases can be achieved by modulating their size, shape, surface charge, and surface modifications. This approach provides strategies for enhanced accumulation of NPs in hepatic cells, which will dramatically improve the nanotherapeutic status in liver diseases. The interactions between NPs and liver cells demonstrate the potential for NPs in clinical treatment in the future.

Xiandi Meng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Ge Zhu: Visualization, Data curation. Yong-Guang Yang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tianmeng Sun: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
